texts.orig: ABSTRACT: The most common method of carbon dioxide (CO2) capture is the absorption of CO2 into a falling thin fi lm of an aqueous amine solution. Modeling of mass transfer during CO2 absorption is an important way to gain insight and understanding about the underlying processes that are occurring. In this workanewsoftwaretool has been used to model CO2 absorption into aqueous piperazine (PZ) and binary mixtures of PZ with 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) or methyldiethanolamine (MDEA). The tool solves partial di ff erential and simultaneous equations describing di ff usion and chemical reaction automatically derived from reactions written using chemical notation. It has been demonstrated that by using reactions that are chemically plausible the mass transfer in binary mixtures can be fully described by combining the chemical reactions and their associated parameters determined for single amines. texts.orig: The capture, reversible release, and storage of carbon dioxide (CO2) from combustion fl ue gases (postcombustion capture, PCC) is now recognized by government and industry as a viable near-term option for greenhouse gas abatement. 1,2 It is particularly relevant to electricity generation from fossil fuels (coal, oil, and gas) which accounts for approximately 25% of global CO2 emissions, 3 with this fi gure set to increase drastically in the next 25 years. 4 PCC has a number of practical advantages over other methods such as oxy- fi ring to produce a pure CO 2 stream and IGCC with precombustion capture and is of similar economic cost. 5 In particular, PCC can be retro- fi tted to existing power stations and integrated into new ones. Additionally, the parasitic energy demand of a PCC plant on a power station can be reduced (at the cost of CO2 removal e ffi ciency) when additional output is required during times of peak load or optimal electricity pricing.

texts.orig: BASF, and MHI). In a packed absorber column fl ue gas (entering from the bottom) and amine solution (entering from the top) are counter-currently contacted at atmospheric pressure and low temperature ( ∼ 40 ° C). The CO2 rich amine solution is then circulated to the top of a desorption column which is at high temperature ( ∼ 120 ° C). At this temperature the chemical equilibria are shifted such that the absorbed CO2 is released and exits at the top of the column. The regenerated amine solution is then circulated back to the absorber column.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work a software tool has been developed that allows the description of chemical reaction and di ff usion in a falling thin fi lm. The software tool allows chemistry of any complexity to be entered using chemical equations and automatically derives the underlying mathematical model. The software tool has been designed in such a way as to allow mass transfer to be predicted from a minimum of chemical and physical data in a fairly rigorous way. It will be shown that if the chemical reactions are chosen prudently, with consideration of chemical plausibility, an amine mixture can be described from the chemistry of its component amines without the need to introduce additional reactions. This is an important point as it goes toward the ability of the model to capture the underlying chemical and physical behavior and therefore extrapolate beyond the speci fi c conditions of a single data set. The amines considered were PZ and PZ mixtures with 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) and methyldiethanolamine (MDEA).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A software tool has been implemented in the Matlab 15 programming environment that describes di ff usion and chemical reaction in a falling thin liquid fi lm exposed to a gas. This has been achieved by solving the partial di ff erential equations and nonlinear simultaneous equations that de fi ne the di ff usion, reaction and equilibrium processes occurring in the fi lm as a function of time and distance. Axial dispersion and heating e ff ects are neglected and the equations are solved in one dimension perpendicular to the gas -liquid interface. It is also assumed that physical properties such as viscosity and density remain invariant within the fi lm and as a function of absorbed CO2.

Axial dispersion and heating e ff ects are neglected and the equations are solved in one dimension perpendicular to the gas -liquid interface. It is also assumed that physical properties such as viscosity and density remain invariant within the fi lm and as a function of absorbed CO2.

texts.orig: where Di is the di ff usion coe ffi cient of species i (m 2 s -1 ), c i is the concentration of species i in molarity (M), x is the distance from the gas -liquid interface (m), t is time (s), and r i is the rate of formation or destruction of i by chemical reaction (M 3 s -1 ). The Matlab function pdepe, 18 which relies on the method of lines, was used to numerically solve the complete system of partial di ff erential equations for a particular chemical system assuming slab geometry. The computation time of pdepe is dependent upon the number of grid points chosen for the space dimension. In all calculations completed for this work 50 grid points with a logarithmic distribution was chosen. This combination yielded accurate results by ensuring closely spaced grid points near the gas -liquid interface, while minimizing the computation time (typically ∼ 30 s on a standard laptop with 2.4 GHz Intel processor). 100 linearly spaced grid points was chosen for the time dimension.

texts.orig: The reaction rate r i consists of ordinary di ff erential equations whose de fi nition is a function of the chemical reactions that occur in the liquid. Any fast equilibria, such as protonation equilibria, where equilibration can be considered instantaneous relative to kinetically de fi ned reactions need to be coupled to these di ff erential equations. This is necessary to provide the complete speciation of the chemical system, which is required to solve the ordinary di ff erential equations. The speciation in a system of equilibria can be solved by fi nding the roots of nonlinear simultaneous equations. This is done by solving the equilibria using the Newton -Raphson method within the pdepe function each time calculation of the derivatives is required. Extensive details of how the di ff erential and simultaneous equations are constructed and solved is given in the Model Details section of the SI.

texts.orig: As concentrated amine solution are being considered (up to 5 mol 3 L -1 ) in this work the concentrations of ions can become large at high CO2 loadings. As a consequence it is necessary to take into account non ideal behavior due to changes in activity of charged species. In the calculation of the di ff erential and simultaneous equations the values of rate and equilibrium constants are corrected for ion activity by calculating activity coe ffi cients ( γ i ) with Speci fi c Ion Interaction Theory (SIT) as given in eq 2 and using activities ( ai = c i γ i ) rather than concentrations.

If this is done the results for single amine systems can be combined to predict the behavior of binary amine mixtures without the need for additional reactions 12,14 or unusual transport phenomena such as the ' shuttle mechanism ' . 19 The chemical reactions that occur between CO2 and water (H2O) have been extensively studied and are well documented. They can be fully described by the following reactions.

texts.orig: In aqueous amine solutions a number of additional reactions between CO2, primary and secondary amines and amine protonation need to be considered. Normally one of two reaction mechanisms is used to described the direct reaction between CO2 and a primary or secondary amine (R1R2NH) to form a carbamate (R1R2NCOO -), known as the zwitterion and termolecular mechanisms. 7 They are both derived from a detailed mechanism in which CO2 and an amine react to form an intermediate complex from which a proton is then removed via a base. This mechanism is illustrated in Figure 1.

In reality this e ff ect can be neglected because at pH values and concentrations relevant for CO2 capture the product K BcH þ is essentially invariant. The e ff ect of the presence of a base with a di ff erent value of K B is canceled by a corresponding change in the value of cH þ . Aparity plot of the product K BcH þ for a simple acid -base equilibrium yields all the points falling on or close to y = x. The value of lg K B was varied between 9.5 and 8.5 (this covers the range of values used in this work) and the ratio of acid to base was varied between 0.002 and 0.98. This plot is shown in Figure S1 of the SI. For a given acid -base ratio the value of K BcH þ does not vary signi fi cantly with the magnitude of K B. This allows the value of k 0 Am, -1 determined in single amine systems to be used in amine mixtures provided the protonation equilibria of all amines are included.

To validate the chemical reactions used the reactions for CO 2 -H2OandCO2 -PZ, eqs 3 -8 and eqs 13 -17, respectively, were used to predict the CO2 -PZ -H2O equilibrium data given in Ermatchkov et al. 21 at 313 K. In Ermatchkov et al. ' s work mixtures of CO 2 -PZ -H2Owere prepared and equilibrated and the speciation determined by 1 H NMRspectroscopy. There is large variation in the PZ concentration and the CO2 loading (mol CO2/mol amine) in this data set. The ordinary differential equations describing the chemical reaction rates were solved in time until equilibrium was reached. The space dimension was not considered. The interaction coefficients of eq 2, which was used to calculate the activity coefficients, were initially set to zero. A surprising result was that without any optimization of the interaction coefficients, and using the specified literature values for all reaction rate and equilibrium constants, the agreement with the literature data is excellent. These results are shown in a parity plot given in Figure S2 of the SI with the speciation in terms of total PZ ( c PZ þ c PZH þ þ c PZH2 2 þ ), total PZCOO -( c PZCOO -þ c HPZCOO -) and total PZ(COO)2 2-( c PZ(COO)2 2 -). The largest deviation between the literature data and the model prediction is 30% with the vast majority of points within 5%. The average absolute deviation expressed as a percentage (AAD%) is 8.1. With this result as a basis, the interaction coefficients were set to zero when modeling the mass transfer data.

It is surprising considering that all interaction coe ffi cients were set to zero in the SIT model used to estimate ion activity coe ffi cients (see the SI for model details). The implication is that for the PZ system, and as will be seen later the other mixtures considered in this work, the short-range interactions that the interaction coe ffi cients describe do not produce signi fi cant deviations from what is already captured by describing the long-range electrostatic interactions.

This model was then used to predict the CO2 absorption flux data as given in Samanta and Bandyopadhyay. 12 This work covered PZ concentrations of 0 to 0.94 M and AMP concentrations of 2.49 to 3.33 M, temperatures of 298 to 313 K, and CO2 partial pressures of 1.93 to 14.06 kPa. texts.label: text
texts.orig: One advantage of a chemically rigorous model is that it allows investigation of the reason behind the synergistic e ff ect of an amine mixture on the absorption fl ux. At comparable conditions of temperature, PZ concentration and CO2 partial pressure, a PZ-AMP mixture has a larger absorption fl ux than PZ in isolation. 11,12 The reason becomes clear if the concentration pro fi les in the fi lm at the same conditions with and without AMP are compared. Figure 5 shows a plot of selected model predicted concentration pro fi les in a fi lm of either 0.23 M PZ and 3.14 M AMP or 0.23 M PZ in isolation under the same conditions. The role of AMP is that it acts to preferentially accept protons over PZ. This occurs because although PZ and AMP share similar p K a values (9.46 and 9.29 respectively at 313 K) the fact that AMP is present at higher concentration means it accepts a greater proportion of the protons. The e ff ect of this is that more unreacted PZ remains in solution enhancing mass transfer. In the presence of AMP the PZ concentration at the gas -liquid interface after 0.44 s exposure to CO 2 is 0.11 M. Without AMP it is 0.045 M. AMP is essentially acting as a pH bu ff er, maintaining a higher pH as CO2 is absorbed. This is illustrated in Figure S1 of the SI where the pH in the solution with AMP is calculated to be more than one unit larger throughout most of the fi lm relative to the solution containing PZ only.
